step_1_context.text: When we report something that was said or thought in the past, we often use a past tense in the reported clause:

step_2_explanation.simple_rule:
When we report something that was said or thought in the past, we often use a past tense in the reported clause:
 • Yusuf said that he didn't want anything to eat.
step_2_explanation.formula:
When the situation described is a permanent / habitual situation, we can use either a present or past tense if the reporting verb is in the past:
 • She argued that Carl is / was the best person for the job.
step_2_explanation.common_mistake:
We can use a present tense for the reporting verb when we report current news, opinions, or information that we have heard but don't know if it's true:
 • I hear you’re unhappy with your job.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 35.1.1 'I've never been in love with Oliver.' $ightarrow$ She ___ that she ___ in love with Oliver.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Use past perfect or past simple in the that-clause after denied.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 35.1.2 'I think the vase is around 250 years old.' $ightarrow$ She ___ that the vase ___ around 250 years old.
35.2.1 Engineers hope that they ___ the problems with the bridge now that new supports have been built.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Present perfect continuous/simple after 'hope' for present relevance.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 35.2.2 The current law ___ that an employee has the right to appeal.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Laws usually use present tense reporting verbs.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 35.3.1 Protesting that he ___ at home all day. (Nokes)
